g.data-narration: Welcome to Relational Algebra - the mathematical foundation for querying databases. Let us explore this essential topic together.
g.text: Examples of Queries in Relational Algebra

g.data-narration: Relational Algebra provides mathematical operations to retrieve, manipulate, and transform data in databases using tables called relations.
g.text: A theoretical foundation of RDBMS providing set of mathematical
operations to manipulate relations (tables) for data retrieval.

g.data-narration: Understanding relational algebra helps you write SQL queries, optimize database performance, and grasp how databases process requests internally.
The six fundamental operators are Selection, Projection, Union, Set Difference, Cartesian Product, and Rename. These form the building blocks.
Selection (σ) filters rows based on a condition. It returns all tuples satisfying the specified condition, keeping all attributes.
Projection (π) selects specific columns from a relation, eliminating duplicate tuples. It performs vertical partitioning of a table.
Set operations combine relations: Union (∪) merges, Difference (-) finds unique to first, and Intersection (∩) finds common tuples.
g.text: Tuples in R but not in S

g.data-narration: Cartesian Product (×) creates all combinations between two relations. Rename (ρ) assigns new names to avoid ambiguity in self-joins.
Natural Join (⋈) combines relations based on common attribute names, performing equijoin and removing duplicate common columns.
g.text: Syntax: R ⋈ S (joins on common attribute names)

g.data-narration: Theta Join (⋈θ) combines based on any condition. Equijoin is a special case using only equality (=) in the condition.
Division (÷) returns tuples from the first relation that match ALL tuples in the second relation - solves for-all type queries.
g.text: Syntax: R ÷ S (returns tuples in R matching ALL tuples in S)

g.data-narration: Query 1: Find names of employees earning more than 50000 using selection (σ) followed by projection (π).
g.text: Query: Find names of employees earning more than 50000

g.data-narration: Query 2: Find department names using natural join between EMPLOYEE and DEPARTMENT tables on Dept_ID.
g.text: Example 2: Department Names via Natural Join
Query: Find department names for all employees
Join result: Combined tuples with matching Dept_ID

g.data-narration: Outer joins preserve tuples that do not have matching values - left keeps all from left, right from right, full from both.
Key takeaways: Use σ for filtering rows, π for selecting columns, ⋈ for joining tables, and ÷ for for-all queries. Practice builds mastery.
g.text: ✓ Selection (σ) → filters rows | Projection (π) → selects columns
✓ Natural Join (⋈) → joins on common names | Theta Join → joins on any condition
✓ Division (÷) → solves "for all" queries | Outer joins preserve unmatched tuples
✓ R ⋈θ S = σθ(R × S) | R ∩ S = R − (R − S)
